front.title: Security Patterns for Constrained Device Onboarding over Unauthenticated Local Transports

abstract.t: Constrained devices are frequently onboarded using local transports that provide confidentiality but limited or no peer authentication, such as Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) "Just Works" pairing or proprietary short-range radios. While these transports are often described as "secure", they do not provide device identity guarantees, and naive onboarding designs frequently conflate encrypted channels with authenticated peers.

This document describes common failure modes, threat models, and design patterns for bootstrapping trust in constrained devices over encrypted but unauthenticated local transports. It does not define a new protocol. Instead, it documents design patterns identified through analysis of constrained-device onboarding requirements, including delegation of onboarding authority, handling of unavoidable race conditions, bootstrap message semantics, and transition to steady-state secure communication. The goal is to provide guidance to designers of constrained device onboarding systems and to clarify the security properties such systems can and cannot achieve.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: Constrained devices such as sensors, actuators, and embedded controllers are commonly onboarded into a larger system using short-range local transports. These transports are attractive because they are widely available, energy-efficient, and often supported by commodity user devices such as smartphones.

In practice, many such transports provide confidentiality and integrity for messages exchanged between two endpoints, but do not provide strong guarantees about the identity of the peer during initial contact. Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) pairing using the "Just Works" association model is a common example: the resulting link is encrypted, but is vulnerable to active man-in-the-middle attacks during pairing and does not provide meaningful peer authentication.

A recurring design error in onboarding systems is the assumption that an encrypted local channel implies that the communicating peer is the intended device. This assumption leads to protocols that implicitly rely on properties the transport does not provide, resulting in race conditions, identity confusion, or brittle security checks layered on top of the channel.

At the same time, many real-world systems intentionally accept these limitations in exchange for usability, cost, or power- consumption constraints. Rather than attempting to eliminate all attacks -- which is often infeasible -- such systems instead aim to bound the impact of attacks, ensure recoverability, and concentrate trust decisions in components capable of enforcing policy.

This document captures design patterns and common failure modes for constrained-device onboarding systems that operate over encrypted but unauthenticated local transports. It focuses on threat-model-first reasoning, explicit separation of bootstrap and steady-state security properties, and the use of delegated authorities during onboarding. The document does not propose new cryptographic mechanisms or replace existing standards such as EDHOC , OSCORE , or Bluetooth Security Manager Protocol (SMP) ; instead, it complements them by clarifying the assumptions required for safe composition.

The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

section.name: Terminology
section.t: This document uses the following terms:

dl.dd: The entity that serves as the root of trust for a deployment. It stores device credentials, validates enrollment requests, and enforces policy. Examples include a local gateway, a network controller, or a cloud service.
A device with limited processing, memory, power, or connectivity resources that is the subject of onboarding. Before onboarding, the device has no pre-existing identity or credentials.
A user-operated device (typically a smartphone) that mediates onboarding on behalf of the authorization authority. The mobile agent holds a revocable credential issued by the authority.
The communication channel used between the mobile agent and the constrained device during onboarding (e.g., BLE, NFC, proprietary short-range radio).

The communication channel used between the constrained device and the authorization authority after onboarding (e.g., a low-power radio protocol, Wi-Fi, or cellular). This may be the same as or different from the local transport.
The process by which a constrained device is registered with the authorization authority and provisioned with credentials for steady-state communication.
The initial phase of enrollment during which no shared credentials exist between the device and the authority.
The operational phase after enrollment, during which the device and authority share a symmetric key and can communicate with mutual authentication.

section.name: Scope
section.t: This document addresses the design space of onboarding protocols for constrained devices that meet ALL of the following criteria:

ul.li: The device has no factory-provisioned identity (no IDevID, no manufacturer certificate, no pre-installed keys).
The local transport provides confidentiality (encryption) but does not reliably authenticate the peer during initial contact.
A human operator is involved in the onboarding decision, either directly (pressing a button, scanning a code) or through a delegated mobile agent.
The device is too constrained -- in power, memory, or connectivity -- to perform multi-round key exchange protocols or maintain persistent TLS/DTLS sessions.
An authorization authority serves as the root of trust for the deployment and makes all final enrollment decisions.

section.t: The guidance in this document is transport-agnostic. While examples reference BLE and short-range radio protocols, the patterns apply to any local transport where encryption does not imply authentication.

section.name: Non-Goals
section.t: This document does NOT:

ul.li: Define a new protocol, message format, or wire encoding.
Replace or compete with EDHOC , OSCORE , BRSKI , ACE-OAuth , or any other IETF standard.
Provide a formal security proof or verification. The patterns described are based on analysis of onboarding requirements and informal threat modeling.
Address firmware update security (see SUIT ).
Address post-onboarding application-layer encryption or access control.

section.name: Relationship to Existing Standards
section.t: Several IETF standards address aspects of constrained-device security. This document addresses a gap that none of them individually fill:

dl.dd: BRSKI provides automated, voucher-based onboarding but requires factory-provisioned X.509 identity certificates (IDevID) and a Manufacturer Authorized Signing Authority (MASA). This document addresses systems where devices have no factory-provisioned identity and no manufacturer is in the trust chain.

EDHOC provides a compact, formally analyzed authenticated key exchange. However, it assumes the communicating parties already have a basis for mutual identification (e.g., pre-provisioned credentials or certificates). This document addresses the prior step: how to establish that basis when the device is anonymous.

ACE provides authorization for constrained environments using OAuth 2.0 semantics. It assumes the authorization server already knows which clients and resource servers exist. This document addresses the bootstrap of that knowledge.

OSCORE provides end-to-end object security for CoAP. It assumes keys already exist (from EDHOC, DTLS, or out-of-band provisioning). The patterns in this document are complementary: they describe how to establish keys that OSCORE (or similar protocols) can subsequently use.

section.t: The patterns in this document compose well-understood cryptographic primitives and are designed to be compatible with adoption of the above standards when the system's constraints permit.

section.name: Common Failure Modes
section.t: This section describes design errors that commonly arise in constrained-device onboarding systems that operate over encrypted but unauthenticated local transports.

section.name: Conflating Encryption with Authentication
section.t: The most common failure mode is treating an encrypted channel as proof of peer identity. When a BLE "Just Works" connection is established, the link is encrypted and integrity-protected, but the pairing process does not verify which device is at the other end.

Systems that rely on the encrypted channel for identity make an implicit assumption: "because this channel is encrypted, the device I am communicating with is the one I intend." This assumption is violated by any active attacker who can initiate or intercept a pairing session.

The consequence is that credential material transmitted over the channel (such as a device key or enrollment payload) may be delivered to an attacker rather than the intended device. The system has no mechanism to detect this substitution.

section.name: Trusting the Agent to Validate
section.t: A second failure mode occurs when the authorization authority delegates validation to the mobile agent. If the authority accepts an enrollment request solely because the agent submitted it -- without independently verifying the agent's credential or the request's integrity -- then a compromised or rogue agent can enroll arbitrary devices.
Mitigation: the authorization authority MUST independently validate every enrollment request, including verification of the agent's credential and any integrity checks on the enrollment payload.

section.name: Permanent State from Unverified Bootstraps
section.t: A third failure mode occurs when a device commits to permanent state changes (e.g., erasing a one-time enrollment payload) based on an unverified response. If an attacker can forge an enrollment acknowledgement, the device may believe it is enrolled when the authority has no record of it. If the device has already erased its enrollment payload, it cannot retry and is permanently stranded.
Mitigation: defer irreversible state changes until the device has independently verified enrollment through normal authenticated communication with the authority (see ).

section.name: Threat Model Framework
section.t: Onboarding protocols that operate over encrypted but unauthenticated transports face a specific set of threats that differ from those addressed by transport-layer security protocols. This section establishes a framework for documenting trust boundaries, assumptions, and explicit limitations.

section.name: Trust Boundaries
section.t: Systems in this design space typically involve four classes of entity, each with distinct trust properties:

dl.dd: Root of trust for the deployment. Stores all device credentials, validates all enrollment requests, and enforces policy. Assumed to operate in a controlled environment with a protected key store.
Initially has no identity or credentials. After onboarding, authenticated via a unique per-device symmetric key. Trusted for its own data once enrolled, but has no authority over other devices.
Holds a revocable credential issued by the authorization authority. Trusted to initiate enrollment, but the authority independently validates every request the agent submits.
Provides confidentiality but NOT peer authentication during initial contact. Treated as untrusted for identity purposes.

section.t: A critical design principle is that the authorization authority MUST NOT delegate its validation responsibilities to the mobile agent. The agent initiates enrollment; the authority decides whether to accept it.

section.name: Core Assumptions
section.t: The following assumptions underlie the design patterns in this document:

ul.li: The authorization authority is operated in a controlled environment with a protected key store.
Device identity is bound to an embedded symmetric key. Physical access to the device is equivalent to key compromise.
The authority has no persistent outbound connection to devices. Communication is always device-initiated.

The mobile agent's authority derives from a credential issued by the authorization authority. The agent generates per-device keys and submits enrollment requests, but the authority independently validates every request.
Compromise of a mobile agent's credential enables unauthorized enrollment of new devices, but does not affect devices already enrolled (each has an independent key).

section.name: Documenting Explicit Non-Goals
section.t: Systems operating in this design space SHOULD explicitly document which threats they do NOT address. Common examples include:

dl.dd: Many constrained-device protocols authenticate messages (via MAC or signature) but do not encrypt payloads. If the application requires confidentiality, it MUST be provided at a higher layer.
If the authority's key store is compromised, all device keys are exposed. This is an operational security problem, not a protocol problem.
Radio jamming, BLE interference, and similar physical-layer attacks are outside the scope of application-layer onboarding protocols.

section.t: The value of explicitly documenting non-goals is that reviewers and auditors can evaluate the system against its stated threat model rather than against an implicit (and often unrealistic) assumption of universal security.

section.name: Bootstrap Versus Steady-State Security
section.t: A common source of confusion in constrained-device protocols is the failure to distinguish between bootstrap security (the properties available during initial onboarding) and steady- state security (the properties available after credentials have been established).

section.name: The Bootstrap Problem
section.t: During bootstrap, the constrained device has no pre-existing credentials. The onboarding protocol must establish a shared secret between the device and the authorization authority, using only a local transport that does not authenticate peers.

This creates a fundamental asymmetry: the authorization authority can verify the mobile agent's identity (via the agent's credential), but neither the authority nor the agent can cryptographically verify the identity of the constrained device during first contact. The device is, by definition, anonymous until onboarding completes.

Protocols that attempt to solve this problem by layering additional key exchanges on top of the unauthenticated transport are solving a different problem. The core issue is not the absence of a key exchange mechanism; it is the absence of any prior basis for knowing WHICH device is being communicated with.
Effective bootstrap designs therefore focus on:

ul.li: Concentrating trust decisions in the authorization authority.
Ensuring that the consequences of a compromised bootstrap are bounded and recoverable.
Making the human operator's intent (pairing THIS device) as explicit as the transport allows.

section.name: Dual-Transport Architectures
section.t: In many constrained-device systems, the local transport used for onboarding (e.g., BLE) differs from the steady-state transport used for ongoing communication (e.g., a low-power radio protocol). This dual-transport architecture arises naturally when:

ul.li: The constrained device lacks the resources to maintain a persistent BLE connection but can transmit short radio frames.
The authorization authority is reachable over the steady- state transport but not over the local transport.
The mobile agent bridges the two transports during onboarding but is not involved in steady-state operation.

section.t: In such architectures, the constrained device relays an opaque enrollment payload from the local transport to the authorization authority over the steady-state transport. The device does not interpret the payload and cannot forge one.
The design patterns in and accommodate both single-transport and dual-transport architectures.

section.name: Session Lifecycle: A Reference Pattern
section.t: This section describes one pattern for session management that is well-suited to sleep-based constrained devices. Other patterns exist (e.g., persistent counters with flash wear- leveling, epoch-based schemes, or challenge-response per message). The pattern described here optimizes for zero persistent state on the device.

ol.li: The device wakes from deep sleep and transmits a session- initiation message containing a fresh random nonce generated by a hardware random number generator.
The authorization authority responds with an authenticated message that includes a random starting sequence number, binding the response to the device's nonce.
All subsequent messages in the session use incrementing sequence numbers derived from the authority-assigned starting value.
The device completes its work and enters deep sleep. Neither the device nor the authority persists any replay- protection state across sleep cycles.

section.t: This pattern has two important properties: it requires no persistent sequence counters (avoiding flash-wear on constrained devices), and it binds each session to a fresh random nonce (preventing cross-session replay).
Alternative patterns include:

ul.li: Persistent monotonic counters stored in flash with wear- leveling. Suitable for devices with sufficient flash write budget.
Epoch-based schemes where the authority maintains a coarse-grained epoch counter and rejects messages from previous epochs.
Per-message challenge-response, where each message includes a fresh challenge. Suitable for low-frequency communication but doubles message count.

section.name: Replay Protection Without Persistent State
section.t: Traditional replay protection requires at least one party to maintain a monotonically increasing counter across sessions. This is impractical for devices that deep-sleep between every communication cycle and have limited flash-write budgets.
The reference pattern described in achieves replay protection through a combination of:

ul.li: A fresh random nonce per session (generated by the device).
A random starting sequence number per session (assigned by the authority in an authenticated response).
Strictly incrementing sequence numbers within a session.

section.t: An attacker who replays a captured session-initiation message causes the authority to create a new session with a DIFFERENT random starting sequence. The attacker cannot generate valid follow-up messages because they lack the device's symmetric key to compute the message authentication code. Replayed post-initiation messages from a previous session carry stale sequence numbers and are rejected.

section.name: Why Session-Initiation Replay Is Tolerable
section.t: Session-initiation messages can themselves be replayed by an attacker. This is tolerable because:

ul.li: The attacker cannot forge authenticated follow-up messages without the device's symmetric key.
The authority creates a new session that simply times out with no further activity. No state is corrupted.
The real device's next wake cycle creates an independent session unaffected by the phantom.

section.t: The operational cost is limited to a spurious log entry and possibly a wasted command to a device that is not listening. Both are self-correcting.

section.name: The Mobile Agent Pattern
section.t: In many deployments, the authorization authority is not directly accessible to the constrained device during onboarding. A mobile agent bridges this gap.
The mobile agent pattern involves three phases:

dl.dd: The mobile agent registers with the authorization authority and receives a unique agent credential (e.g., a 256-bit pre-shared key). This is a one-time operation per agent.

The mobile agent generates a unique per-device credential, constructs an enrollment request authenticated with its agent credential, encrypts the request with the authority's public key, and transmits both the device credential and the encrypted enrollment request to the constrained device over the local transport.

The constrained device relays the encrypted enrollment request to the authorization authority over the steady-state transport (see ). The authority decrypts the request, verifies the agent's authentication, extracts the device credential, and registers the device.

section.t: The following diagram illustrates the message flow:

figure.artwork: Mobile Constrained Authorization Agent Device Authority | | | | Phase 1: Agent Registration | |------------------------------>| | agent credential issued | |<------------------------------| | | | | Phase 2: Device Provisioning | | device_cred + encrypted_req | |--------------->| | | | | | Phase 3: Authority Validation| | | relay(enc_req)| | |------------->| | | enroll_ack | | |<-------------| | | |

section.t: The constrained device acts as a relay for an opaque, encrypted payload. It does not interpret the enrollment request and cannot forge one.

section.name: Agent Credential Lifecycle
section.t: Agent credentials SHOULD support the following operations:

dl.dd: The authority generates a unique credential and transmits it to the agent over a secured channel.
The agent authenticates every enrollment request with its credential. The authority verifies the credential before accepting any request.
The authority operator can revoke an agent credential at any time. Revoked credentials are retained for audit purposes, but all future requests authenticated with the revoked credential are rejected.

section.name: Trust Properties of Delegation
section.t: A well-designed delegation model provides the following properties:

dl.dd: Only agents holding a valid (non-revoked) credential can enroll new devices.
Each agent has a unique credential. One agent cannot forge requests as another.
The authority records which agent credential was used to enroll each device.
Revoking an agent credential immediately disables that agent's enrollment authority.
The agent credential is a symmetric pairing credential. It does not grant access to the authority's private key, device key database, or other authority state.

section.name: Registration Windows
section.t: To prevent unauthorized agents from silently enrolling by connecting to the authority's local interface, the authority SHOULD implement a registration window:

ul.li: Agent registration requests are rejected unless a registration window is currently open.
The window is opened only by explicit operator action (e.g., a physical button press or an authenticated administrative command).
The window closes automatically after a configurable duration (e.g., 120 seconds).

section.t: This ensures that agent registration requires both possession of a valid local transport connection AND contemporaneous operator intent.

section.name: Trust-On-First-Use for Authority Identity
section.t: When a mobile agent connects to an authorization authority for the first time, there is typically no pre-existing trust anchor. An attacker could present their own keypair and produce a valid response.
The recommended approach is Trust-On-First-Use (TOFU) with key pinning:

ol.li: The agent establishes that the authority holds a specific public key (e.g., via a challenge-response signature, a displayed fingerprint, or a QR code).
The agent persists the authority's public key and a stable authority identifier (pin-on-first-use).
Subsequent connections reject any authority presenting a different public key.

section.t: Specific mechanisms for establishing the initial binding include:

ul.li: Manual verification: the operator compares a fingerprint of the authority's public key displayed on both devices.
Out-of-band channel: the authority's public key is transferred via QR code, NFC tap, or USB.

section.t: For environments requiring stronger assurance than TOFU, the operator SHOULD verify the authority's public key fingerprint out-of-band before relying on the pinned key.

When using challenge-response, including the authority identifier in the signed payload binds the signature to a specific authority instance, preventing replay of a signature obtained from a different authority.

section.name: Handling Unavoidable Races
section.t: When the local transport does not authenticate peers, certain race conditions are inherent and cannot be eliminated by protocol design alone. The goal is to detect, bound, and recover from these races rather than pretend they do not exist.

section.name: The Transport-Layer MITM Race
section.t: If the local transport uses an unauthenticated pairing model (e.g., BLE "Just Works"), an active man-in-the-middle attacker can intercept the device credential during provisioning. The attacker can then race the legitimate device to submit an enrollment request to the authorization authority.
This race is INHERENT to the transport model and cannot be eliminated without upgrading to an authenticated pairing method (e.g., BLE Passkey Entry or Numeric Comparison).
Systems that accept this risk SHOULD implement the mitigations described in Sections through to bound the impact.

section.name: One-Time-Use Registration Payloads
section.t: The authorization authority SHOULD enforce that each device identifier can be registered at most once. If an attacker wins the race and registers first using the intercepted credential, the duplicate registration attempt from the legitimate device is rejected.
This does not prevent the attack, but it ensures that:

ul.li: The attacker's registration "burns" the device identifier. The attacker cannot register additional devices with the same payload.
The operator can detect the conflict (two parties claiming the same identifier) and investigate.
The legitimate device can be factory-reset and re-provisioned with a new identifier and credential.

section.name: Timestamp-Bounded Replay Windows
section.t: Enrollment requests SHOULD include a timestamp set by the mobile agent. The authorization authority SHOULD reject requests whose timestamps fall outside a tolerance window (e.g., +/-24 hours).
This limits the window during which a captured enrollment request can be replayed, but does not prevent same-day attacks. It is a defense-in-depth measure, not a primary mitigation.

section.name: Binding Acknowledgements to Requests
section.t: When the authorization authority acknowledges a successful enrollment, the acknowledgement SHOULD be cryptographically bound to the specific enrollment request. An example pattern is:
where:

ul.li: device_key is the newly provisioned per-device key.
context_string is a fixed, protocol-specific string that prevents cross-protocol confusion.
enrollment_payload is the exact bytes of the encrypted enrollment request that was relayed by the device.

section.t: The device verifies the registration_proof before accepting the acknowledgement. This prevents an attacker who intercepted the device credential from forging a substitute acknowledgement for a different enrollment.

Other binding mechanisms (e.g., a signature over a hash of the enrollment payload) are equally valid. The essential property is that the acknowledgement is tied to the specific enrollment request the device relayed.

section.name: Self-Healing from Forged Acknowledgements
section.t: Even with bound acknowledgements (), an attacker who intercepted the device credential over the local transport has all the material needed to compute a valid registration_proof.
To handle this case, the device SHOULD implement deferred payload erasure:

ol.li: After accepting a registration_proof, the device sets a "registration complete" flag but does NOT immediately erase the enrollment payload.
On its next wake cycle, the device attempts normal authenticated communication with the authorization authority.
If the authority responds with a valid authenticated message (proving it knows the device's key and has the device in its registry), the device erases the enrollment payload permanently.
If the authority does not respond or responds with an authentication failure, the device clears the "registration complete" flag and reverts to enrollment mode on its next wake cycle.

section.t: This self-healing behavior ensures that a forged acknowledgement does not permanently strand the device in a state where it believes it is enrolled but the authority has no record of it.

section.name: Identity Destruction and Re-Creation
section.t: In many constrained-device systems, the appropriate response to key compromise is not key rotation but identity destruction. This is a deliberate design choice, not a limitation.
When a device's symmetric key is compromised:

ol.li: The authorization authority removes the compromised device from its registry.
The device is factory-reset, which erases: the symmetric key (identity), any persistent application state, and any resident program or configuration.
After reset, the device is inert -- it cannot authenticate with any authority and will not execute application logic.
The operator re-provisions the device via the normal onboarding flow, establishing a new key and a new identity.

section.t: This destroy-and-recreate model has several advantages over in-place key rotation:

ul.li: Simplicity: no key-rotation protocol is needed on the constrained device.
Clean state: any application state that may have been influenced by the attacker is also erased.
Auditability: the new identity is a distinct entry in the authority's registry, making it clear that a re-provisioning event occurred.

section.t: The cost is operational: an operator must physically interact with the device (or be in BLE range) to re-provision it. For systems where this is acceptable, it is a simpler and more robust approach than managing key rotation on a constrained device.

section.name: Security Considerations
section.t: This entire document is about security considerations for constrained-device onboarding. This section highlights three cross-cutting concerns.

section.name: Silent Discard
section.t: Protocols operating over unauthenticated transports SHOULD handle all authentication and integrity failures by silent discard. No error response should be sent to the sender.
Silent discard prevents information leakage that could assist an attacker:

ul.li: No indication of whether a key-lookup hint matched any known device.
No indication of whether a message authentication code was close to valid.
No indication of whether the authority has an active session for the claimed device.
No indication of whether the message was syntactically well-formed.

section.t: Constrained devices operating in sleep-based architectures already expect message loss due to radio unreliability. Silent discard is therefore compatible with existing retry logic (await response or timeout, then retry or sleep).

section.name: Key Compromise Containment
section.t: The per-device, per-agent keying model described in this document provides natural compromise containment:

dl.dd: Limited to the compromised device. Other devices are unaffected. The authority removes the compromised device from its registry. The device can be factory-reset and re-provisioned with a new key (see ).

Enables the attacker to forge enrollment requests for new devices, but does NOT affect devices already enrolled (each has an independent key). The operator revokes the compromised credential; all future requests authenticated with it are rejected. Devices enrolled before revocation remain valid unless individually removed.

section.t: These properties depend on each device and each agent having a unique, independent key. Shared or derived keys weaken containment.

section.name: Residual Risks
section.t: The patterns in this document reduce but do not eliminate risk. Designers SHOULD acknowledge the following residual risks in their threat models:

ul.li: TOFU () is vulnerable to an active attacker during first contact between the mobile agent and the authorization authority. Out-of-band verification of the authority's public key fingerprint mitigates this risk.

BLE "Just Works" pairing (or equivalent unauthenticated transport pairing) permits active MITM interception of device credentials. For high-threat environments, an authenticated pairing method (Passkey Entry, Numeric Comparison, or OOB) is required.

The authorization authority is a single point of compromise. If its key store is breached, all device keys are exposed. Operational security measures (encrypted storage, access control, backup) are essential but outside the scope of protocol design.

The patterns in this document compose well-understood cryptographic primitives (ECDH, AEAD, HMAC, CBOR) but have not been subjected to formal verification. Designers who require provable security guarantees should consider formally analyzed protocols such as EDHOC for the key-exchange component, while recognizing that such protocols do not address the full onboarding problem described here.

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This document has no IANA actions.
